name: Mehrangarh Fort
shortDescription: One of India's largest forts, rising 125 metres above the 'Blue City' of Jodhpur.
description: Mehrangarh Fort rises dramatically 125 metres above Jodhpur, its massive sandstone walls up to 36 metres thick making it virtually impregnable. Founded by Rao Jodha in 1459, it houses seven magnificent gates, each commemorating a military victory. Within its walls lie ornate palaces with latticed windows and sweeping views of the blue-painted city below. The fort's museum contains one of India's finest collections of Mughal armour and palanquins.

name: Jaisalmer Fort
shortDescription: The 'Golden Fort' — a living desert fortress where a quarter of the city still lives.
description: Rising from the Thar Desert like a vision from the Arabian Nights, Jaisalmer Fort is one of the world's few living forts — approximately 3,000 people still reside within its walls. Built from local yellow sandstone in 1156 by Rawal Jaisal, it blends seamlessly with the desert landscape. Its 99 bastions, palaces, Jain temples, and narrow lanes preserve medieval Rajput life in extraordinary continuity.

name: Red Fort
shortDescription: Shah Jahan's red sandstone Mughal palace-fortress — the symbolic heart of India.
description: Built by the Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan as the palace of his new capital Shahjahanabad, the Red Fort is enclosed by 2.41 km of imposing red sandstone walls up to 33 metres high. Its palaces, pavilions, and gardens represent the zenith of Mughal architecture. Since independence, India's Prime Minister has addressed the nation from its ramparts on Independence Day each August 15th, making it the symbolic heart of the republic.

name: Amber Fort
shortDescription: A magnificent hilltop Rajput-Mughal fort reflected in Maota Lake below Jaipur.
description: Rising in tiered concentric rings above Maota Lake, Amber Fort is the finest example of the fusion between Rajput and Mughal architecture. Built by Raja Man Singh I and expanded over 150 years, its Sheesh Mahal (Hall of Mirrors) — whose ceiling is encrusted with thousands of tiny mirror tiles — is one of the most dazzling interiors in India. Elephants once carried royalty up the zigzagging ramp to the main gate.

name: Great Wall at Mutianyu
shortDescription: The best-preserved section of the Great Wall, with 22 watchtowers in 2.25 km.
description: The Mutianyu section of the Great Wall is among the best-preserved and most scenic stretches of the entire structure. The current fortification was built in 1368–1644 during the Ming Dynasty to protect Beijing from Mongol and Jurchen incursions from the north. Dense with 22 watchtowers per 2.25 km stretch, its battlements, garrison stations, and signal towers represent the pinnacle of Chinese military engineering.

name: Golconda Fort
built: c. 13th century; Qutb Shahi expansion 16th–17th c.
shortDescription: A massive diamond-trading citadel with an acoustic marvel — a hand-clap heard a kilometre away.
description: Golconda was one of the world's greatest diamond markets — the Koh-i-Noor, Hope Diamond, and Regent Diamond all came from its nearby mines. The Qutb Shahi kings expanded it into a massive granite fortress complex on a 120-metre hill with three lines of walls and 87 bastions. Its most famous feature is an acoustic system where a hand-clap at the main gate can be heard at the top of the citadel a kilometre away.

name: Chittorgarh Fort
shortDescription: The largest fort in India, the pride of Rajput chivalry and site of three great johar (mass immolations).
description: Chittorgarh is the largest fort in India, covering 700 acres on a 180-metre mesa above the Rajasthan plains. The symbol of Rajput pride and resistance, it was the capital of Mewar and withstood three famous sieges — each ending in the ritual mass immolation (johar) of the Rajput women rather than surrender. The tower of Vijay Stambha (Victory Tower), decorated with sculpted figures on all nine stories, is one of India's finest medieval monuments.

name: Angkor Thom
shortDescription: The last great capital of the Khmer Empire — a walled city of a million inhabitants.
description: Angkor Thom ('Great City') was the last capital of the Khmer Empire, built by Jayavarman VII around 1181. Enclosed by a 12-km moat and 8-metre-high walls, it once housed up to one million people — making it perhaps the largest pre-industrial city on earth. Its centrepiece is the Bayon temple with its 216 serene stone faces. The south gate's causeway lined with giants holding a serpent is one of the world's most dramatic entrances.

name: Sacsayhuamán
shortDescription: Inca ceremonial fortress above Cusco with colossal stones fitted without mortar.
description: Sacsayhuamán — whose name may mean 'satisfied falcon' — is a massive Inca ceremonial and military complex overlooking Cusco. Its three tiered zigzag walls are built from enormous limestone, andesite, and diorite blocks, the largest weighing 360 tonnes. The stones are fitted so precisely that a sheet of paper cannot be inserted between them. The Spanish used its stones to build colonial Cusco; what remains is still staggering.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of City of Cusco)
